coredata.dc:title: Sludge carbon quantum dots-activated peroxymonosulfate oxidation for sludge conditioning

coredata.dc:description:
Large amounts of waste activated sludge (WAS) are produced from biological treatment processes in wastewater treatment plants, and its effective disposal and reutilization are considered to be inevitable challenges in the world. In this work, carbon quantum dots derived from WAS (SCQDs) were successfully synthesized via hydrothermal method, which had a significant photoluminescence effect, excellent water solubility, and graphite structures with main elements of C, N, and O. Subsequently, its potential for peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation under visible light for WAS conditioning was investigated. The results showed that SCQDs-activated PMS (SCQDs-PMS) system could effectively improve sludge dewaterability. Under experimental conditions, capillary suction time reduction and water content of sludge cake after conditioning were 81.2% and 72.6%, respectively. Reactive oxygen species including
S
O
4
· -
,
·
OH
, and
1
O
2
produced in SCQDs-PMS system could effectively degrade extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) (especially tyrosine-like and tryptophan-like substances in tightly bound-EPS), inactivate microbial cells, and destruct α-helix structure of protein molecule, which resulted in the desirable release of EPS-bound water, consequently improving sludge dewaterability. This result was well supported by the variations in the transverse relaxation time of protons at low field nuclear magnetic resonance. Thus, this work explored a potential pathway of reutilizing sludge and a new activator for PMS activation. However, it should be noticeable that this work was the first attempt using SCQDs-PMS system for sludge conditioning, the future studies about system optimization and its wider application scenarios should be further investigated.

head.ce:title: Sludge carbon quantum dots-activated peroxymonosulfate oxidation for sludge conditioning

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Large amounts of waste activated sludge (WAS) are produced from biological treatment processes in wastewater treatment plants, and its effective disposal and reutilization are considered to be inevitable challenges in the world. In this work, carbon quantum dots derived from WAS (SCQDs) were successfully synthesized via hydrothermal method, which had a significant photoluminescence effect, excellent water solubility, and graphite structures with main elements of C, N, and O. Subsequently, its potential for peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation under visible light for WAS conditioning was investigated. The results showed that SCQDs-activated PMS (SCQDs-PMS) system could effectively improve sludge dewaterability. Under experimental conditions, capillary suction time reduction and water content of sludge cake after conditioning were 81.2% and 72.6%, respectively. Reactive oxygen species including
S
O
4
· -
,
·
OH
, and
1
O

produced in SCQDs-PMS system could effectively degrade extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) (especially tyrosine-like and tryptophan-like substances in tightly bound-EPS), inactivate microbial cells, and destruct α-helix structure of protein molecule, which resulted in the desirable release of EPS-bound water, consequently improving sludge dewaterability. This result was well supported by the variations in the transverse relaxation time of protons at low field nuclear magnetic resonance. Thus, this work explored a potential pathway of reutilizing sludge and a new activator for PMS activation. However, it should be noticeable that this work was the first attempt using SCQDs-PMS system for sludge conditioning, the future studies about system optimization and its wider application scenarios should be further investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
With the rapid development of urbanization and the fast improvement of environmental consciousness in the world, large amounts of waste activated sludge (WAS) with high water content and huge volume are generated from biological treatment processes in wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) [1], which still continue to increase due to the increasing demand for sanitation [2]. In 2021, the total number of sewage treatment facilities in municipal cities and counties were 2827 in China, which produced 14.23 million tons of dry WAS [3]. Sludge management is widely considered as the inevitable challenge for WWTPs in both economic and environmental sides for the following reasons. On the one hand, the management cost of sludge is very expensive and even up to 50 − 60 % of the total operation cost of WWTPs [4,5]. On the other hand, the possible second environmental pollution will be occurred if sludge disposal is not appropriate [1]. Before sludge disposal, dewatering is a key step because it can effectively minimize sludge volume and reduce landfill leachate, as well as be favorable for sludge transportation [2]. Nowadays, different kinds of methods are developed for improving sludge dewaterability, mainly including physical treatment (e.g., freezing/thawing, microwave irradiation, and ultrasonication), chemical treatment (e.g., flocculation/coagulation, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), and acid/alkali conditioning), biological treatment (e.g., bioleaching, bioflocculation, and enzyme addition), and their combinations [6–13]. Among these methods, AOPs have been attracted great attentions from academic and industrial communities due to the highly conditioning efficiency contributed from the strong oxidation capacity of radical species [1,7,8]. In the past decade, sulfate radical (
S
O
4
· -
)-based AOPs (SR-AOPs) have been widely applied for enhancing sludge dewaterability [7,8], because
S
O
4
· -
has a high redox potential (2.5 − 3.1 V), a wide operation pH range (3.0 − 8.5), and a long lifetime (30 − 40 μs) [14]. In addition, the precursors of
S
O
4
· -
generate non-toxic products in sludge system after conditioning [7]. Compared to peroxydisulfate (PDS,
S
2
O
8
2-
), peroxymonosulfate (PMS,
H S
O
5
-
) attracts more popularity and the generation of
S
O
4
· -
from its activation is relatively easier because of its asymmetric structure [15].

At present, the commonly-used activators for PMS mainly include transition metals and their derivates, heat, microwave, ultraviolet, metal–organic frameworks, and carbon materials [8,16]. Among these activators, carbon materials are cost effective and environmentally friendly [17]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as one of carbon materials are gradually and tentatively applied for PMS activation for the degradation of organic pollutants due to its photo-induced electrons transfer and excellent semiconductor properties, but CQDs are always modified before use in present studies [17–19]. CQDs have many merits, such as high photo-stability, good water dispersibility, low fabrication cost, and excellent biocompatibility, which make it with great potentials applied in pollution treatment fields, such as photo-catalysis, sensitizer, heavy metals detection, and adsorption treatment [17,20]. In general, raw materials used for fabricating CQDs are always different kinds of natural carbon sources, such as dried leaf, grass, and citric acid [17]. Recently, the attempts to use sewage sludge and excess sludge from a paper mill to synthesize CQDs have been made, but its further possible applications remain underexplored [21,22]. WAS contains amounts of organic substances and various elements (e.g., C, N, and O), thus it is inherently helpful for the heteroatom doping during the synthesis process of CQDs from WAS (SCQDs), and the diverse functional groups (e.g., amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups) might be formed on its surfaces, which will be in favor of enhancing its fluorescence quantum yield [22]. It is noticeable that the synthesis of CQDs from WAS is desirable and interesting, because it might find a new way to reutilize WAS.

Although the feasibility of PMS activation by CQDs has been demonstrated to be efficient and environmentally friendly during the degradation processes of organic pollutants [17–19], the potential application of SCQDs-activated PMS (SCQDs-PMS) system for sludge conditioning is not clear yet. In addition, the activators for PMS activation used for sludge conditioning at present mainly focus on iron and its derivates, heat, and microwave [8,23,24]. Therefore, SCQDs were firstly synthesized from WAS in this work using hydrothermal method, and its potential application for PMS activation under visible light for WAS conditioning was investigated. The physicochemical properties of the synthesized SCQDs were characterized. In addition, the effects of the synthesis solid–liquid ratio and dosages of SCQDs and PMS on sludge dewaterability were investigated. After conditioning, the physicochemical properties of sludge were analyzed. The aim of this work was to tentatively explore a new pathway for sludge to achieve the concept of “using waste to treat waste”. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt using SCQDs-activated PMS (SCQDs-PMS) system for sludge conditioning.

Raw sludge (RS) was from a sedimentation tank of a municipal WWTP located in Fuzhou, China, which was kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C and used within 3 d. Before experiments, sludge temperature naturally elevated to room temperature and its supernatant was poured off. Meanwhile, sludge was filtered using a 0.6 mm screen with the aim to remove impurities with large particle size. The main characteristics of sludge were listed in Table S1. In addition, all chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Firstly, sludge sample was dried at 105 °C in an oven to constant weight and ground to powders, which were sieved using a 100 mesh screen to obtain the precursor of SCQDs. Secondly, as shown in Fig. S1, 2.0 g of sludge was transferred into the deionized water at a preset synthesis solid–liquid ratio (0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.1) and stirred to obtain uniform slurry, which was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave with a volume of 50 mL for hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 12 h. In view of the safety and heat resistance limit of the conventional reactor, this autoclave was selected as the high temperature reactor in this work. After reaction, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was ultrasonicated for 1 h and then centrifuged at 10,000 g for 1 h. Lastly, the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm organic filter membrane and freeze-dried to obtain SCQDs powders. In addition, the synthesis process of SCQDs derived from the treated WAS (TSCQDs) by SCQDs-PMS system was the same as the above-mentioned process.

200 mL of sludge sample was firstly added into a glass beaker with a volume of 500 mL. When the preset dosages of PMS (100, 200, 300, 400, 500 mg/g dry solids (DS)) and SCQDs (200, 250, 300, 350, 400 mg/g DS) were added into the beaker, sludge was stirred at 200 rpm for 15 min to mix sufficiently and irradiated under visible light using two 300 W Xe lamps (λ > 420 nm). The effect of reaction time on SCQDs-PMS system for sludge conditioning was shown in Fig. S2, which suggested that the suitable reaction time was 15 min. Further increasing reaction time had a slightly negative impact on sludge dewaterability. The similar phenomenon was observed in previous work using PMS activation oxidation for sludge conditioning [5]. In addition, the change of sludge pH during conditioning process was shown in Fig. S3. Other comparable systems including SCQDs-PMS, single PMS, and single SCQDs were performed at dark and under visible light, and the dosages of PMS (200 mg/g DS) and SCQDs (350 mg/g DS) were the same as those for SCQDs-PMS system obtained under optimal conditions. The reaction time for all systems was 15 min. TSCQDs activated-PMS (TSCQDs-PMS) system was also carried out under the optimal conditions for SCQDs-PMS system. For the radical quenching experiments, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), ethanol (EtOH), and furfuryl alcohol (FFA) were used as the scavengers for
·
OH
, both
S
O
4
· -
and
·
OH
, and
1
O 2
on the basis of the previous studies [19,25], respectively. After conditioning, two different dosages (10 and 50 mM) of scavengers were added in SCQDs-PMS system and used to quench the reactive oxygen species (ROS). Subsequently, an electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR, JES-FA200, JEOL Ltd) was used to characterize ROS. In this work, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used to capture both
S
O
4
· -
and
·
OH
, while 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP) was used to capture
1
O
2
.

Contamination factor (CF) of heavy metals (HMs) including Cu, Zn, Cr, and Pb was investigated to assess the ecological risk of RS, SCQDs, and sludge after conditioning by SCQDs-PMS system. CF was the ratio of HM concentration in the sample to its reference value. The reference values of Cu, Zn, Cr, and Pb are 17.16, 62.80, 83.74, and 34.65 mg/kg [26], respectively.

The morphology, lattice structure, surface functional groups, and surface element morphology of SCQDs were characterized by the transmission electron microscopy equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (TEM-EDS, Thermo Scientific, Talos F200S), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker, D8 ADVANCE), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, Ltd, China), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, Escalab 250Xi), respectively.

Capillary suction time (CST) was measured by a CST instrument (304 M, Triton 126 Electronics Ltd.). CST reduction was obtained according to the following equation [27]. The determination of water content of sludge cake (Wc) was in line with the standard method [28].
(1)
CST reduction =
CST 0
-
CST t
CST 0
× 100 %
where CST0 and CSTt are the values of CST before and after conditioning by SCQDs-PMS system, respectively.

Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured with a TOC analyzer (TOC-VCPH, Shimadzu, Japan). Soluble chemical oxygen demand (SCOD), total solids (TS), and volatile solids (VS) were in line with the standard methods [28]. The transverse relaxation time (T2) of protons at low field nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and fluorescence quantum yield of SCQDs were determined according to the methods described in previous studies [2,22]. Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEM) spectra of sludge samples were obtained by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600, Hitachi Ltd., Japan) and characterized by the fluorescence region integration (FRI) method. In addition, the detailed descriptions of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) extraction and its FT-IR analysis, cell viability measurement, rheological test, and T2 determination were shown in Supplementary materials.

As shown in Fig. S1, the solution derived from WAS via hydrothermal synthesis method was dark brown under sunlight, which was same as CQDs solution derived from sewage sludge reported recently by Rossi et al. [21]. However, it manifested bright green luminescence under 365 nm UV light, owning the same luminescent effect as other CQDs reported in previous studies [18,29,30]. This photoluminescence effect is an important characteristic for distinguishing CQDs with other carbon nanomaterials [30]. Therefore, the results suggested that SCQDs were successfully synthesized and showed excellent water solubility.

In order to characterize the morphology and element distribution of SCQDs, TEM and XRD measurements were conducted. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the synthesized SCQDs were spherical and distributed uniformly with a diameter of 20 − 30 nm. Meanwhile, SCQDs had two obvious fluorescence peaks (Fig. 1b), suggesting that it was characterized by double excitation centers [22]. The two excited peaks were at 235 and 325 nm, respectively, and their maximum emission wavelengths were both at 405 nm, which was in good agreement with bright green luminescence under 365 nm UV light. In general, CQDs derived from biomass materials always have one excitation center, while the double excitation centers of SCQDs were observed in here. This phenomenon suggested that SCQDs might have two luminescence centers or a single luminescence center with two maximum excitation wavelengths [22]. The similar result was recently observed in CQDs derived from excess sludge from a paper mill WWTP [22]. XRD pattern of SCQDs (Fig. 1c) shows one dominant peak at 30°, which can be assigned to the reflection of graphite structure with high crystallinity [31]. The TEM-EDS mappings (Fig. 1d − 1f) indicate that the main elements of SCQDs are C, N, and O.

To further explore the chemical components of functional groups of SCQDs, FT-IR, UV–vis, and XPS analyses were performed. As shown in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a), the peak at 3367 nm was from N − H stretching vibration, while the peaks at 1567, 1392, 1108, and 610 nm were from C = C, C − N, C − O, and − NH2 groups [22], respectively. The UV–vis adsorption spectroscopy (Fig. 2b) further demonstrated the presences of C = C and C = O bonds in SCQDs, because two different broad bands at 265 and 323 nm were resulted from the π − π* and n − π* transitions of C = C and C = O bonds [32], respectively. Compared to the surface structure of RS, different functional groups especially N-containing groups were observed in SCQDs. It should be noted that the energy gap transmission of π-electron system in SCQDs was the intrinsic reason for photoluminescence emission [33]. Certainly, the functional groups in SCQDs can also capture some of the light excitons and contribute to photoluminescence phenomenon. In addition, the oxygen-functional groups in SCQDs were the origin of high solubility in water and also a source of surface defects [34]. XPS measurement showed that three sharp peaks at 530.22, 398.73, and 284.35 eV (Fig. 2c) were O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. The results suggested that the main elements of SCQDs were O, N, and C, which were in line with the results obtained by TEM-EDS measurement.

High-resolution XPS further showed that C1s band spectrum (Fig. 2d) of RS was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.25, 285.62, and 287.79 eV, corresponding to C = C, C − OH, and carbonyl (−C = O) [22,35,36], respectively. The three peaks at 284.25, 285.82, and 287.77 eV in C1s band spectrum of SCQDs presented C = C, C − N, and − C = O bonds [22], respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2d, it is clearly known that the peak areas of C = C and − C = O bonds of SCQDs are much bigger than those of RS. This suggested that compared to RS, a considerable amount of C = C bonds were generated in SCQDs that were consistent with its graphite structure [22]. Meanwhile, the obvious formation of − C = O bonds and the disappearance of C − OH bonds were ascribed to the dehydration condensation during the hydrothermal synthesis process. As for N1s band spectrum of SCQDs (Fig. 2e), the peaks at 399.65 and 400.04 eV were both contributed from pyridinic nitrogen (C − N − H), while the peak at 401.30 eV was from graphitic nitrogen (N − H) [37,38]. The apparent increase in nitrogen content suggested that SCQDs were characterized by green emission [34]. Graphitic nitrogen was ascribed to the N-doping within the core of SCQDs, while pyridinic nitrogen could be present in the core or on the surface of SCQDs, leading to the defects and consequently increasing the fluorescence yield [36]. In addition, hydrogen bonds with water could be effectively formed due to pyridinic nitrogen with unshared electron pair, resulting in the excellent water solubility of SCQDs observed in this work [22]. O1s band spectrum of SCQDs showed two peaks at 531.48 and 532.20 eV (Fig. 2f), corresponding to C − O and C = O bonds [35], respectively, while the two peaks at 531.81 and 532.39 eV in O1s band spectrum of RS represented C − O and C − O − C bonds [22]. Compared to O1s band spectrum of RS, the disappearance of C − O − C bonds and the formation of C = O bonds in O1s band spectrum of SCQDs suggested that C − O − C bonds were transformed into C = O bonds via hydrothermal process.

According to the above-mentioned discussion and previous studies [21,22], it was speculated that the SCQDs synthesized in this work were composed of graphite inner core and amorphous O-containing outer shell. During hydrothermal synthesis process, RS components firstly occurred aromatization reactions, leading to the production of aromatic clusters. Subsequently, aromatic clusters at a critical point concentration will undergo explosive nucleation and then result in carbonization and dehydration processes [22], producing sheet graphene frameworks via self-assembling, which could be well supported by the significant formation of C = C bonds (Fig. 2d). Meanwhile, the appearance of amides (−NH2) (Fig. 2a and 2d) in SCQDs suggested that carboxyl and ester groups in RS underwent intermolecular/intramolecular dehydration and polymerization processes [22]. With the prolongation of hydrothermal reaction, pyridinic nitrogen (Fig. 2e) can be produced via the further dehydration of some amides with adjacent hydroxyl groups. Graphitic nitrogen (Fig. 2e) was generated via the entrance of some amides into graphene frameworks, the presence of which could be in favor of improving fluorescence quantum yield of SCQDs [36]. Once graphite inner core was formed, the residual fragments with hydrophilic or O-containing groups in the solution could produce surface functional groups in the outer shell, which was well supported by the distinct formation of − C = O bonds in SCQDs (Fig. 2a, 2d, and 2f).

The potential application of SCQDs-PMS system for improving WAS dewaterability under visible light was investigated in this work. The effects of the synthesis solid–liquid ratio and the dosages of SCQDs and PMS on sludge dewaterability are shown in Fig. 3 . It is clearly known that high solid–liquid ratio and overdosing SCQDs and PMS were not in favor of dewatering sludge. The maximum CST reduction and the minimum Wc were 81.2 % and 72.6 %, respectively, which were achieved at 0.04, 350 mg/g DS, and 200 mg/g DS of solid–liquid ratio, SCQDs dosage, and PMS dosage, respectively. Table 1 lists the improvement of sludge dewaterability after conditioning by traditional Fenton oxidation and different PMS/PDS activation systems. It is clearly known that the dewatering efficiency of SCQDs-PMS system is comparable to that of Fenton oxidation and PMS/PDS activation systems using iron, heat, and microwave as activators [5,39–46].

As shown in Fig. 3a, suitable synthesis solid–liquid ratio of SCQDs was in favor of improving sludge dewaterability, while high solid–liquid ratio played a negative role. The reasonable explanation for this was that the lower fluorescence quantum yield of SCQDs was obtained at higher synthesis solid–liquid ratios (Fig. S4), leading to the lower activation efficiency of PMS and consequently achieving lower dewatering performance. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence quantum yield of SCQDs was 15.2 % at the solid–liquid ratio of 0.04, which had a decreasing trend with increasing solid–liquid ratio. The similar phenomenon was also reported in a recent study [22]. The fluorescence quantum yield of SCQDs was desirable and comparable to that of CQDs derived from forest and agricultural biomass [47], and was also higher than those of CQDs derived from waste paper and orange waste peels [48,49]. When SCQDs dosage was lower than 350 mg/g DS, sludge dewaterability was enhanced gradually with increasing SCQDs dosage (Fig. 3b). This suggested that increasing SCQDs dosage was helpful for the photooxidation process by providing more reactive sites and further producing more ROS (Eqs. (2−11), listed in the next section), which could effectively degrade EPS [8]. As a result, EPS-bound water was released efficiently, which led to the significant improvement of sludge dewaterability [5,8,50]. But, sludge dewaterability was deteriorated by further increasing SCQDs dosage. The possible reason for this might be that overdosing SCQDs could produce light-shielding effect and inhibite PMS activation via reducing the utilization of light [51], consequently decreasing ROS production. The similar phenomena were also observed in the earlier studies about the degradation of organic pollutants by CQDs-activated PMS oxidation [18,52]. Compared to SCQDs, PMS had a similar impact on sludge dewaterability. At the beginning, increasing PMS dosage was conducive to improving sludge dewaterability while overdosing PMS deteriorated dewaterability. This is a widely-observed experimental phenomenon when using PMS activation for sludge conditioning [5,39,40]. PMS addition will significantly elevate ROS production while excessive PMS will produce the quenching effect of ROS, especially for
S
O
4
· -
[8]. In addition, the above-mentioned results suggested that there was a reasonable match between PMS dosage and the photogenerated electrons from SCQDs [52], leading to produce enough ROS to improve sludge dewaterability.

According to the above-obtained experimental conditions, sludge dewaterability after conditioning via different systems was compared at SCQDs and PMS dosages of 350 and 200 mg/g DS, respectively. As shown in Fig. S5, a low CST reduction of 22.0 % was achieved after treatment by SCQDs-PMS system at dark, which was similar to those after treatment by single PMS addition at dark or under visible light. In addition, CST reduction was 12.0 % after treatment by single SCQDs addition (Fig. S5), suggesting the limited enhancement of sludge dewaterability. The similar results were also observed in previous studies about organics degradation [19,29]. The possible reason for this was that ROS was not produced in the absence of oxidant [19]. However, a significant increase of CST reduction was 81.2 % when SCQDs-PMS system was applied under visible light. In previous studies, the degradation of organic pollutants by CQDs-PMS system at dark was also very limited, which only could be significantly enhanced under light irradiation [18,29,52]. The results suggested that light irradiation for SCQDs-PMS system was indispensable, because SCQDs can effectively adsorb light and activate PMS to generate ROS [52]. ROS formation could efficiently degrade EPS and effectively release the bound water, thus sludge dewaterability was significantly improved [8].

To further explore the specific contributions of ROS to the enhancement of sludge dewaterability in terms of CST reduction, the classical quenching experiments were carried out. In order to ensure ROS quenched completely, two different dosages of scavengers were added in SCQDs-PMS system after conditioning. As shown in Fig. S6a, no signal was observed when 10 mM FFA was added in SCQDs-PMS system, suggesting that
1
O
2
was completely quenched. But, relatively weak characteristic signals of DMPO-
S

and DMPO-
·

and

Thus, the dosages of FFA and TBA used for the classical quenching experiments were chosen to be 10 mM and EtOH dosage was 50 mM, which was similar to those reported in previous studies [53–55]. As shown in Fig. S8, CST reduction after treatment by SCQDs-PMS system under visible light was 81.2 %, which slightly decreased to 69.5 % in the presence of FFA. When TBA was added into SCQDs-PMS system, CST reduction was 56.4 %, which further significantly declined to 20.7 % in the presence of EtOH. The results suggested that

The results also suggested that except for the predominant ROS oxidation effect, there were other processes affecting slightly sludge dewaterability, such as minor ROS (e.g.,

and

) and direct PMS oxidation [8,56].

To explore the underlying explanations for the improved dewaterability, the physicochemical properties after conditioning via SCQDs-PMS system were analyzed in this work. As shown in Fig. 5 a, non-Newtonian behavior was observed because of non-linear relationships between shear rates and shear stress for before and after treatment samples [57]. Meanwhile, the apparent viscosity and shear stress after conditioning by SCQDs-PMS system were both lower than those before conditioning, and the decrease of apparent viscosity accompanied with the increase of shear rate, suggesting shear-thinning behavior [57]. It is well known that sludge rheological behavior is closely linked to its micro-structures [58]. Therefore, the change of rheological behavior suggested that sludge flocs were deformed. The production of strong oxidative
S
O
4
· -
,
·
OH
, and
1
O
2
in SCQDs-PMS system could effectively degrade EPS and might destroy a part of microbial cells, leading to the linkages breakage of sludge structures [8,50]. As a result, the decrease of flow resistance resulted in the enhancement of sludge fluidity and the decline of sludge viscosity.

Water occurrence states of sludge play an important role in sludge dewatering process. In view of different affinities between water and its surrounding mediums, T2 of protons at low field NMR can be used to characterize water mobility [59]. In general, the longer T2 indicates the lower bind energy and the higher water mobility, while the shorter T2 suggests the closer affinity of water with its surrounding mediums [60]. As depicted in Fig. 5b, four peaks were observed in the T2 distribution spectra, suggesting that protons had four different states including T21, T22, T23, and T24, which represented the solid-form protons, vicinal water, interstitial water, and water with the least effect by solid compositions [59], respectively. T22 and T23 account for the total content of bound water in sludge. It is clearly known that T22, T23 and T24 peaks located at 1.0, 7.8, and 72.3 ms for RS shifted right to 1.3, 9.7, and 102.3 ms for the treated sludge, respectively. This phenomenon suggested that the produced ROS in SCQDs-PMS system significantly reduced the bind energy between water and sludge flocs, and consequently facilitated the removal of sludge water [61]. In recent studies, different chemicals such as Fenton reagent, cationic polyacrylamide (CPAM), KMnO4, polymeric aluminum chloride (PACl) were pointed out that they had different impacts on water states in sludge, thus leading to different dewatering efficiencies [59,61,62].

Figs. S9a and S9b are the CLSM images of sludge before and after treatment by SCQDs-PMS system, respectively. It is clearly known that most regions of CLSM image for RS were green, of which for the conditioned sludge were red. The results suggested that RS flocs contained amounts of living microbial cells, which were significantly inactivated after treatment by SCQDs-PMS system. To quantitively describe cell viability, the live/dead ratio of cells was determined and showed in Figs. S9c and S9d. The live/dead ratio of cells for RS sample was 258.8 %, which significantly declined to 28.6 % for the conditioned sludge. This phenomenon suggested that SCQDs-PMS system might destruct a part of microbial cells in sludge flocs and further release intracellular water [63,64], because ROS including
S
O
4
· -
and
·
OH
could penetrate the cell walls of microbials and destroy the uniformity of cell walls [24]. Recently, Wu et al. [65] demonstrated that the mobile water fraction was lower than 2 % even though the complete lysis of microbial cells in sludge was achieved. Thus, the possible lysis of microbial cells reasonably had very limited efficiency in dewatering sludge [65]. In addition, as shown Fig. S9e, TOC and SCOD in TB-EPS fraction decreased significantly from 64.2 and 825.2 mg/L to 15.3 and 266.1 mg/L, respectively, suggesting that ROS produced in SCQDs-PMS system played an important role in degrading and even mineralizing organic substances in sludge flocs [5,8]. As a result, the water affinity of organics in TB-EPS decreased apparently and further improved sludge dewaterability [50,65]. The slight increase in TOC content in S-EPS fraction was reasonably ascribed to the release of organics in TB-EPS fraction or even intracellular substances after conditioning by SCQDs-PMS system, and their elution was slightly higher than their mineralization [5,65].

To further explore the impact of SCQDs-PMS system on the functional groups of sludge EPS, FT-IR analysis was carried out in this work. It has pointed out that FT-IR spectrum at 3600 and 600 cm−1 shows the obvious characteristic peaks of functional groups of hydrocarbons, proteins, polysaccharides, and aromatic amino acids in sludge EPS [70]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the peaks at 3324 and 2933 cm−1 were contributed from the stretching vibrations of O − H and C − H bonds of hydrocarbons [71], respectively. The peak at 1644 cm−1 was the vibrations of C − N and C = O bonds associated with proteins (amide I), while the peak at 1525 cm−1 was the vibrations of C − N and N − H bonds in amide II [72]. The peak at 1409 cm−1 was assigned to the symmetric stretching of C = O bonds in the protonated carboxyl groups [53]. The peak at 1040 cm−1 was ascribed to the vibrations of C − OH, C − O − C, and C − C bonds in polysaccharides [72]. The peaks located at 780 and 688 cm−1 were the ring vibrations of C − C and C − OH bonds of aromatic amino acids [71], respectively. Fig. 7b also shows that SCQDs-PMS system did not change the positions of characteristic peaks associated with these functional groups while increased their transmittance, suggesting the degradation of organic substances in sludge EPS. This result was well supported by the declines in the concentrations of proteins and polysaccharides (Fig. 7a).

To obtain more information about the secondary structures of proteins, amide I band from 1700 to 1600 cm−1 in FT-IR spectra was further analyzed by infrared self-deconvolution with second derivative resolution and curve fitting. As shown in Fig. 7c and 7d, six peaks in the range of 1700 to 1600 cm−1 appeared and the relative intensities of the corresponding secondary structures of proteins were listed in Table S2. After conditioning by SCQDs-PMS system, the percentage of α-helix decreased from 22.7 % to 15.6 %. Meanwhile, the ratio of α-helix/(β-sheet + random coil) declined from 49.7 % to 32.8 %, which suggested that the looser structure of proteins was obtained [56]. This phenomenon was in good agreement with those reported by previous studies based on AOPs [53,56,64,69]. The results indicated that SCQDs-PMS system broke the hydrogen bonds, which was the intrinsic reason of the stability of α-helix structure between amide hydrogen of peptide bond number N + 4 and carbonyl oxygen of peptide bond number N [2]. As a result, the exposure of hydrophobic sites of proteins resulted in strong surface hydrophobicity and was further conducive to the enhancement of sludge dewaterability [56].

According to the aforementioned discussion, the primary underlying mechanism for the enhanced dewaterability after treatment by SCQDs-PMS system was that organic substances especially tyrosine-like and tryptophan-like substances in TB-EPS fraction were effectively degraded by strong oxidative
S
O
4
· -
,
·
OH
, and
1
O
2
, which will result in disrupting the secondary structures (especially α-helix) of these macromolecular proteins and further enhancing the surface hydrophobicity. As a result, the effective release of EPS-bound water leads to the improvement of sludge dewaterability.

ce:displayed-quote.ce:simple-para: With the aim to achieve the concept of “using waste to treat waste”, SCQDs was successfully synthesized and used for PMS activation under visible light, which was demonstrated to be an effective alternative for improving sludge dewaterability and did not introduce harmful by-products into sludge. Meanwhile, SCQDs-PMS system could effectively inactivate microbial cells and destruct α-helix structure of protein molecule. In addition, according to the CF values listed in Table S3, the ecological risk levels of Cu, Zn, Cr, and Pb in the synthesized SCQDs decreased remarkably. Thus, the synthesis process of SCQDs effectively removed HMs from RS and further significantly reduced the environmental risk of HMs. After conditioning by SCQDs-PMS system, the ecological risk levels of HMs in the treated sludge were also much lower than those of HMs in RS. In other words, SCQDs-PMS system was in favor of decreasing the ecological risk of HMs in sludge. Under optimal conditions, the operation cost of SCQDs-PMS system for sludge dewatering was estimated to be 212 USD/ton DS (Table S4). As shown in Table S5, it is clearly known that SCQDs-PMS system was much cheaper than traditional Fenton oxidation and iron-activated PDS/PMS systems, while was comparable to electrochemical peroxidation combined with electro-Fenton (ECP-EF) system. Thus, SCQDs-PMS system might be an inexpensive method for sludge conditioning. Nevertheless, a comprehensive cost analysis will be necessary to scale up SCQDs-PMS system to a pilot or full-scale operation.

ce:section.ce:para:
After conditioning by SCQDs-PMS system, CST reduction was comparable to that obtained by other PMS/PDS activation oxidation with commonly-used activators (Table 1) [5,39–46]. In addition, the activation property of TSCQDs was also explored. As shown in Fig. S10, the dewatering efficiency of TSCQDs-PMS system was comparable to that of SCQDs-PMS system, which suggested a potential reutilization way of the treated sludge by SCQDs-PMS system. Meanwhile, the recovery and reuse of SCQDs will be seriously taken into account in future. ROS including
S
O
4
· -
,
·
OH
, and
1
O
2
produced in SCQDs-PMS system could effectively degrade EPS especially TB-EPS and inactivate microbial cells, which would be favorable to the release of EPS-bound water, consequently improving sludge dewaterability. Therefore, this work not only explored a potential pathway of reutilizing sludge, but also found a new method for PMS activation. However, the potential of SCQDs-PMS system used for removing organic pollutants in wastewater should be investigated in future with the aim to explore more application channels. In addition, it should be noted that this work was the first attempt to apply SCQDs-PMS system for improving sludge dewaterability, the further studies including reactor design, parameter optimization, operation cost, experimental scale, SCQDs recovery and reuse, as well as filtrate analysis and treatment should be systematically investigated. In real treatment scenarios, the complicated procedures always restrict their practical applications. Considering SCQDs-PMS system without pH adjustment, complex procedures, and other combinations, these conditions might provide a desirable potential of its practical application.

In this work, SCQDs were firstly synthesized and its potential for PMS activation under visible light for WAS conditioning was explored. The conclusions can be drawn as follows:
(1)
SCQDs have a significant photoluminescence effect, excellent water solubility, and graphite structures with main elements of C, N, and O.
(2)
SCQDs-PMS system could effectively improve sludge dewaterability. The optimal synthesis solid–liquid ratio and dosages of SCQDs and PMS were 0.04, 350 mg/g DS, and 200 mg/g DS, respectively, under which CST reduction and Wc were 81.2 % and 72.6 %, respectively.
(3)
Longer T2 after treatment by SCQDs-PMS system suggested higher water mobility and lower bind energy.
(4)
Predominant ROS were
S
O
4
· -
,
·
OH
, and
1
O
2
in SCQDs-PMS system, which could effectively degrade tyrosine-like and tryptophan-like substances in TB-EPS and inactivate microbial cells.
(5)
Destruction of α-helix resulted in the looser structure of protein molecule and the exposure of sludge surface hydrophobic sites, which facilitated the enhancement of sludge dewaterability.
